readings.title: The Elements of Computing Systems (Nand2Tetris) — Chapter 2: Boolean Arithmetic
readings.note: Covers binary number representation, two's complement arithmetic, half adders, full adders, and the Hack ALU design. The Hack ALU uses just 6 control bits to implement 18 operations — an elegant demonstration of how far minimalist design can go. Read alongside Problem Set 2.

readings.title: IEEE Standard 754-2019 for Floating-Point Arithmetic
readings.note: The definitive reference for floating-point representation. The Wikipedia article provides an excellent overview of single/double precision formats, special values (NaN, infinity, denormals, signed zero), and rounding modes. Understanding IEEE 754 is essential for anyone writing numerical software — floating-point surprises (like 0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3) are among the most common bugs in production systems.

readings.title: Carry-Lookahead Adder and Prefix Adders
readings.note: Detailed slides covering the derivation of CLA equations, the transition from flat CLA to hierarchical prefix adders (Kogge-Stone, Brent-Kung, Han-Carlson), and the area-depth tradeoffs between different prefix structures. Includes gate-level diagrams and delay analysis.

readings.title: Booth's Multiplication Algorithm
readings.note: Covers both standard Booth's (radix-2) and modified Booth's (radix-4) algorithms with worked examples. The radix-4 variant halves the number of partial products and is the standard in modern hardware multiplier implementations. Pay attention to how the algorithm naturally handles signed numbers without special cases.

readings.title: Computer Organization and Design: RISC-V Edition — Chapter 3: Arithmetic for Computers
readings.authors: David A. Patterson and John L. Hennessy
readings.note: The standard textbook treatment of ALU design, overflow detection, and floating-point arithmetic in the context of a real processor architecture (RISC-V). Covers how the ALU connects to the processor datapath — context we will build on in Weeks 5-6.
